title: Introducing Contextual Embeddings
concept: Contextual embeddings generate a word's vector dynamically based on its neighbors, representing different meanings for the same spelling.

exercises.prompt: What do words like 'bat' (animal vs. sports equipment) and 'bark' (tree vs. dog sound) have in common?

options.text: They have different meanings depending on the context.
They always mean the exact same thing.
They cannot be represented by computers at all.

exercises.explain: Words with the same spelling can have completely different meanings depending on their context. Contextual embeddings help computers understand these differences.

cards.title: The Word-Matching Puzzle
cards.body: If you hear the word 'run', do you think of a quick jog, a tear in a stocking, or a political campaign? Without context, humans can't tell, and neither can simple computers. Let's see how modern AI solves this!

cards.title: What are Contextual Embeddings?
cards.body: Contextual embeddings are advanced AI tools that look at the words surrounding a target word. Instead of giving a word a single, permanent list of numbers (vector), it builds a dynamic vector on the spot based on the sentence's meaning.

cards.title: The Shape-Shifting Dictionary
cards.body: Imagine traditional embeddings are like looking up a word in a paper dictionary—the word 'bank' always has the same printed page, no matter what you are talking about. Contextual embeddings are like a smart digital assistant that changes the definition of 'bank' on the screen depending on whether you are talking about money or a river!

cards.title: One Word, Two Meanings
cards.body: Take the word 'apple'. In 'I ate a juicy apple', the system creates a vector representing fruit. In 'I bought a new Apple computer', the system creates a different vector representing a technology company. The same spelling gets two different mathematical representations!

cards.title: Myth: Words Have Fixed Vectors
cards.body: Many people think AI models use a static, unchanging database of word meanings. While older models did this, modern AI models recalculate the meaning of every word from scratch every time they read a new sentence!

cards.title: Visualizing Context
cards.body: See how the same word gets mapped differently based on its neighbors.

left.text: 'Go to the bank to deposit cash.' -> Vector leans towards finance.

right.text: 'Walk along the river bank.' -> Vector leans towards earth/water.

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between static embeddings and contextual embeddings?

options.text: Static embeddings give a word the same vector, while contextual embeddings change it based on surrounding words.
Static embeddings only work with numbers, while contextual embeddings only work with text.
Static embeddings are faster to type, but contextual embeddings are shorter.
There is no difference; they are two names for the same thing.

exercises.explain: Static embeddings use the exact same vector for a word regardless of how it is used, while contextual embeddings change the vector depending on the sentence.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Under a contextual embedding system, the word 'light' in 'light bulb' and 'light feather' will have different vector representations.
exercises.explain: Contextual embeddings generate different vectors for the same spelling based on how the word is used in a sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of dynamic embeddings.
exercises.sentence: To decide the correct vector for a word, contextual embeddings analyze the surrounding text, also known as the ________.
exercises.explain: Contextual embeddings rely on the surrounding words (the context) to determine the exact meaning and mathematical representation of a word.

exercises.prompt: Two of these systems use smart contextual embeddings. Spot the system that is using old-style STATIC embeddings instead!

options.text: System A: Assigns different vectors to 'fly' in 'a tiny fly' and 'birds fly'.
System B: Modifies the vector of 'fly' depending on the nearby verbs.
System C: Gives the exact same vector to 'fly' in both 'a annoying fly' and 'airplanes fly'.

exercises.explain: The third choice is generated by a static embedding system which cannot distinguish between different meanings of the word 'fly' (a bug vs. the action of flying). Contextual embeddings correctly separate them.

exercises.prompt: How does a contextual embedding system figure out whether the word 'bank' means a place for money or the side of a river?

options.text: It searches a pre-made dictionary for all possible definitions of the word.
It forces the user to manually select the correct meaning of the word.
It analyzes the neighboring words to generate a unique vector for that specific sentence.
It ignores the word completely and only looks at punctuation.

exercises.explain: Contextual embeddings dynamically look at surrounding words (like 'river' or 'money') to build a custom mathematical vector for the word 'bank', allowing the computer to understand which meaning is intended.
